prompt: Users are getting 14 separate push notifications when someone dumps 14 comments on their thread in a minute. I want a coalescer that turns that into digests: `coalesce.py`, class Coalescer(window, max_batch). It's driven entirely by caller-supplied timestamps (our scheduler ticks it; in tests we replay recorded traffic), and time may never go backwards across ANY call — same timestamp again is fine, earlier is ValueError and must leave state untouched. add(key, payload, at): the first payload for a key opens that key's window at `at`; more payloads for the key pile into the batch IN ARRIVAL ORDER while the window is open. A batch flushes for one of three reasons: 'window' — its window is window units old (opened_at + window <= now, checked against every call's timestamp BEFORE the new payload is processed, and an expired batch flushes even if the current add is for some other key); 'size' — the batch just hit max_batch payloads, it flushes right there in that add; 'forced' — flush_all(at) pushed everything out (shutdown path). add/drain/flush_all all return the list of batches they flushed, each batch a dict {key, payloads, opened_at, reason}, and when several flush at once they're ordered by opened_at with alphabetical key as the tiebreak. Note the boundary: a payload arriving exactly window after its batch opened does NOT join it — the old batch goes out as 'window' and the payload opens a fresh one. drain(at) just advances time (flushes expired, returns them); pending() maps each open key to its queued count. After any flush the key starts clean at the next payload's own timestamp. Constructor: window a positive number, max_batch a positive int; keys non-empty strings; ValueError everywhere on garbage. The tests in test_coalesce.py are the spec-of-record: `python3 test_coalesce.py` must pass, no edits there.
